statute: NMSA § 60-13-1 et seq. (Construction Industries Licensing Act); NMAC Title 14, Chapter 6
license_label: New Mexico Construction Industries Division (CID) License Number
license_notes: New Mexico requires license for virtually all contractors. Exempt only if annual revenue is under $7,200. Unlicensed = criminal offense; contractor cannot file suit or mechanics lien. Homeowner can demand money back.

mandatory_clauses.label: NM CID license disclosure
Mechanics lien notice
mandatory_clauses.text:
NOTICE OF LIEN RIGHTS

Under state law, contractors, subcontractors, and material suppliers who provide labor or materials for your property and are not paid may have the right to file a mechanic's lien against your property. Even if you pay your general contractor in full, you may still be subject to liens from unpaid subcontractors and suppliers. Protect yourself by obtaining written lien waivers from all parties before making final payment.

mandatory_clauses.label: Federal 3-day right to cancel (TILA)
Not legal advice disclaimer
mandatory_clauses.text: DISCLAIMER: This contract was generated by ContractorDesk as a starting point template. It does not constitute legal advice. Contractors are encouraged to have a licensed attorney review agreements for their specific situation and applicable state requirements.

penalties_for_non_compliance: Project under $5,000: up to 90 days jail, $300-$500 fine. Project over $5,000: up to 6 months jail, fine up to 10% of contract value. No lien rights; homeowner can demand money back.
notes: New Mexico requires license for nearly all contractors. Exempt only if annual revenue under $7,200. Unlicensed contractors cannot file mechanics liens, cannot file lawsuits, and homeowner may demand money back. Testing through PSI. Bond and insurance required. Reciprocity with some states.
